claim: AI Scientist (Lu et al., 2024) demonstrated the first fully autonomous system capable of generating novel research hypotheses, implementing experiments in code, and producing complete scientific manuscripts without human intervention, closing the loop from idea to paper.
reason: The claim describes the original AI Scientist by Lu et al. (2024) as a fully autonomous end-to-end system generating hypotheses, implementing experiments, and producing manuscripts. This matches openalex:W4402952666::k00, which describes the same system with identical capabilities at ~$15/paper.

claim: AlphaFold 3 (Abramson et al., 2024) extended structure prediction to biomolecular complexes including DNA, RNA, and small molecules using a diffusion-based architecture, establishing a new paradigm for AI-driven structural biology that accelerates drug target identification.
reason: The claim describes AlphaFold 3's diffusion-based architecture for biomolecular complex structure prediction. None of the candidates describe AlphaFold 3 or any structure prediction system; the candidates cover unrelated topics (macular degeneration, evaluation protocols, biology benchmarks, hypothesis refinement, description logic, and reasoning generalization).

claim: HypoGeniC (Zhou et al., 2024) showed that iterative hypothesis refinement using LLM agents guided by experimental feedback outperforms single-shot generation, achieving statistically significant improvements on drug discovery and materials science benchmarks.
reason: The claim describes HypoGeniC itself showing that iterative refinement outperforms single-shot generation on drug discovery and materials science benchmarks. No candidate matches this: arxiv:2410.17309v3::k00 describes HypoRefine (which builds on HypoGeniC) evaluated on social-science tasks, not drug discovery or materials science benchmarks. The other candidates describe different systems (ACCELMAT, Robin, MATDESIGN, iterative human-LLM-lab workflow, multi-agent collaboration). None describe HypoGeniC's own evaluation on drug discovery and materials science.

claim: SciAgentBench (2024) introduced a comprehensive benchmark of 338 research-level scientific tasks across 44 diverse disciplines to evaluate LLM agents on authentic scientific problem-solving, finding that top agents solve fewer than 10% of tasks.
reason: The claim describes SciAgentBench with 338 tasks across 44 disciplines and a <10% solve rate. The only candidate sharing the name (arxiv:2602.12984v2::k01) describes a tiered suite of 259 tasks and 1,134 sub-questions with L1-L3 levels — different task count, different structural framing, and no mention of 44 disciplines or the <10% finding. No candidate matches the specific benchmark described in the claim.

claim: MLAgentBench (Huang et al., 2024) provided a standardized evaluation framework for LLM agents performing machine learning research tasks including literature review, experiment design, and iterative refinement, revealing systematic failure modes in long-horizon planning.
reason: The claim describes MLAgentBench as a standardized evaluation framework for LLM agents performing ML research tasks (literature review, experiment design, iterative refinement) with systematic failure modes in long-horizon planning. No candidate matches this specific benchmark. The failure-modes candidates (openalex:W7119558012::k00 and arxiv:2602.15112v2::k02) describe failure modes from different systems/papers, not MLAgentBench specifically.

claim: ResearchAgent (Baek et al., 2024) introduced an iterative multi-agent pipeline in which specialized sub-agents handle literature retrieval, idea generation, and critique, showing that structured agent collaboration yields more novel and feasible research ideas than single-agent baselines.
reason: The claim describes ResearchAgent (Baek et al., 2024), a specific system with iterative multi-agent pipeline having specialized sub-agents for literature retrieval, idea generation, and critique. None of the candidates describe this specific system — the closest is openalex:W4412889809::k03 which also involves multi-agent collaboration in scientific idea generation outperforming single-agent baselines, but it describes a different system focused on alignment with research trends and Science of Science findings, not the specific ResearchAgent architecture with its three specialized sub-agent roles.

claim: Autonomous Chemical Research with Coscientist (Boiko et al., 2024 follow-up work) scaled to multi-step organic synthesis planning and robotic execution, successfully completing reactions that required integrating web search, database queries, and hardware control without human intervention.
reason: The claim describes Coscientist (Boiko et al.) — a system for autonomous chemical synthesis integrating web search, databases, and robotic hardware control. None of the candidates match this specific system. The closest are GALILEO (robotic peptide synthesis) and the bioinformatics pipeline, but these are different systems in different domains. No candidate describes Coscientist or autonomous organic chemistry with robotic execution.

claim: SciCode (Tian et al., 2024) established a rigorous benchmark of 338 scientific coding problems derived from real research papers in physics, biology, and chemistry, finding that frontier models including GPT-4o solve fewer than 5% of multi-step problems requiring domain knowledge integration.
reason: SciCode (Tian et al., 2024) is a specific benchmark of 338 scientific coding problems from research papers. None of the candidates describe this system — they describe different benchmarks (AstaBench, ResearcherBench, CompBioBench, SciAgentGym) or corpora with different scopes and problem counts.

claim: AI peer review systems evaluated in Liang et al. (2024) revealed that GPT-4-based reviewers agree with human reviewers at rates comparable to inter-human agreement on NeurIPS and ICLR submissions, while also identifying systematic biases toward longer, more confident-sounding papers.
reason: The claim combines two findings: (1) GPT-4 reviewers agree with humans at inter-human rates on NeurIPS/ICLR, and (2) systematic biases toward longer, more confident-sounding papers. Candidate W4400734098::k01 captures the agreement-rate finding but omits the distinctive bias finding entirely. No candidate mentions biases toward longer or more confident papers, which is a specific empirical result central to the claim. The closest candidates (W4400734098::k01, W4384261641::k00) match only part of the claim and none captures the full specific contribution described.

claim: OpenScholar (Asai et al., 2024) demonstrated that retrieval-augmented LLM agents grounded in a 45-million-paper corpus outperform GPT-4 on scientific question answering while dramatically reducing hallucination rates, setting a new standard for literature-grounded AI assistants.
reason: The claim describes an agent-based RAG system grounded in a large scientific corpus that outperforms GPT-4 on scientific QA benchmarks while reducing hallucinations. Candidate k02 describes exactly this: an agent-based RAG system for scientific literature that surpasses GPT-4 by 30 percentage points on PubMedQA — the same specific empirical contribution, allowing for paraphrase of the benchmark and margin details.

claim: DiscoveryBench (Majumder et al., 2024) formalized data-driven scientific discovery as a benchmark task requiring agents to form and test hypotheses from structured datasets, revealing that current LLMs fail to reliably distinguish spurious from causal relationships.
reason: The claim describes DiscoveryBench as a benchmark for data-driven scientific discovery where agents form and test hypotheses from structured datasets, revealing LLMs fail on spurious vs causal relationships. Candidate k03 from W4416540539 describes controlled synthetic datasets with varied difficulty where hypothesis discovery rates collapse as complexity grows, directly matching the empirical finding about LLM limitations on hypothesis discovery tasks with structured data.

claim: LLM-based automated literature synthesis (Wang et al., 2024) demonstrated that multi-agent systems can produce systematic reviews of comparable quality to human-written reviews on narrow biomedical topics, as judged by domain experts blinded to authorship.
reason: The claim describes a specific multi-agent system for automated literature synthesis producing systematic reviews comparable to human reviews on narrow biomedical topics (Wang et al., 2024). None of the candidates match this specific system or finding — the candidates cover hypothesis generation, peer review quality, and AI-driven paper writing, but none describe a multi-agent literature synthesis system evaluated on systematic review quality in biomedical domains.

claim: BioPlanner (O'Donoghue et al., 2024) introduced a benchmark and agent system for autonomous biological experimental design, showing that LLM agents can generate valid experimental protocols rated as feasible by wet-lab biologists in over 60% of cases.
reason: The claim describes BioPlanner (O'Donoghue et al., 2024), a specific benchmark and agent system for autonomous biological experimental design with a >60% feasibility rating from wet-lab biologists. None of the candidates match this specific system or benchmark — the candidates cover different systems (Robin, AILA, gene-editing bench, etc.) with no overlap with BioPlanner's specific contribution.

claim: Nobel Turing Challenge framing (Krenn et al., 2025) articulated a concrete long-range research agenda for AI systems capable of making Nobel-Prize-level discoveries autonomously, synthesizing lessons from 2024 agent systems to identify key capability gaps in causal reasoning, embodied experimentation, and scientific communication.
reason: The claim describes the 'Nobel Turing Challenge' framing by Krenn et al. (2025) — a specific long-range research agenda paper. None of the candidates match this; the closest is openalex:W4407806895::k04 (a six-level capability taxonomy), but that is a different framework by different authors, not the Nobel Turing Challenge framing.

claim: Sakana AI's evolutionary model merging approach (2024) showed that evolutionary algorithms can autonomously combine foundation model weights to produce specialized scientific models without gradient-based training, achieving competitive performance on mathematical reasoning benchmarks.
reason: The claim describes Sakana AI's evolutionary model merging approach that combines foundation model weights using evolutionary algorithms without gradient-based training. None of the candidates describe this specific system — the closest is k03 (evolutionary code optimization for infrastructure), but that is about code optimization not model weight merging. The claim has no matching candidate.

claim: SciAgentBench (2024) established a benchmark of 338 real scientific tasks across 44 domains, showing that frontier LLMs solve fewer than 5% autonomously, quantifying the gap between current agents and genuine scientific competence.
reason: The claim describes SciAgentBench, a specific benchmark of 338 tasks across 44 domains showing <5% autonomous solve rate by frontier LLMs. None of the candidates match this specific system/benchmark — the candidates describe different evaluations (data-driven coding tasks, biomedical analysis, ML competition tasks, end-to-end research tasks) with different metrics and scopes.

claim: AI Scientist (Lu et al., 2024) demonstrated the first fully autonomous research pipeline — from idea generation through experiment execution, paper writing, and automated peer review — producing novel machine learning findings without human intervention.
reason: The claim describes the AI Scientist (Lu et al., 2024) as a fully autonomous pipeline covering idea generation, experiment execution, paper writing, and automated peer review. Candidate W4402952666::k00 matches this exactly, describing the same system with the same components including the $15/paper cost detail, distinguishing it from W7140287209 which is a different paper about the same system.

claim: ChemCrow (Bran et al., 2023/2024) showed that integrating 13 chemistry tools into an LLM agent (GPT-4) enabled autonomous planning and execution of multi-step organic synthesis, including successful synthesis of a novel chromophore.
reason: The claim describes ChemCrow, a specific system integrating 13 chemistry tools into GPT-4 for autonomous organic synthesis planning. None of the candidates describe ChemCrow or chemistry-focused tool integration; the closest candidates (BioMedAgent, STELLA) are biomedical multi-agent systems, not the same system.

claim: OpenScholar (Asai et al., 2024) introduced a retrieval-augmented system over 45M open-access papers that outperformed GPT-4 on scientific literature QA by grounding every claim in retrieved passages, reducing hallucination rates by ~50%.
reason: Both describe the same system (OpenScholar/agent-based RAG for scientific literature) that outperforms GPT-4 on science QA benchmarks; the candidate specifically cites GPT-4 being outperformed on PubMedQA, consistent with the claim's core contribution of a retrieval-augmented system over a large open-access corpus that beats GPT-4.

claim: MLAgentBench (Huang et al., 2024) provided a standardized environment for evaluating LLM agents on end-to-end ML engineering tasks, finding Claude-2 and GPT-4 succeed on only ~30% of tasks, with failures concentrated in debugging and iterative experimentation.
reason: The claim describes MLAgentBench (Huang et al., 2024), a specific benchmark for evaluating LLM agents on ML engineering tasks with specific performance findings (Claude-2 and GPT-4 at ~30% success). None of the candidates match this specific system — they describe MLGym, EvoScientist, BioML-bench, MLR-Bench, or a GPT-5-based agent evaluation, all of which are different systems/benchmarks.

claim: Autonomous Chemical Research Agent (Boiko et al., 2023) demonstrated that GPT-4 connected to web search and lab robotics could autonomously design and execute chemical experiments, successfully synthesizing three compounds without human guidance.
reason: The claim describes Boiko et al. 2023's system combining LLMs to autonomously design and execute scientific experiments including chemistry (cross-coupling reactions), which matches candidate W4365211632::k00 describing an Intelligent Agent system combining multiple LLMs to autonomously design, plan, and execute scientific experiments demonstrated on catalyzed cross-coupling reactions in chemistry.

claim: LLM-based hypothesis generation benchmark (Wang et al., 2024) showed that GPT-4 and Claude-3 generate scientifically plausible but rarely novel hypotheses compared to human experts, with novelty scores averaging 30% lower on blind expert evaluation.
reason: The claim describes a specific benchmark study (Wang et al., 2024) comparing GPT-4 and Claude-3 hypothesis generation against human experts with a specific quantitative finding (30% lower novelty scores). None of the candidates match this specific system/study. The closest candidates involve LLM hypothesis generation evaluation but describe different systems, different model sets, and different specific findings.

claim: SciCode (Huang et al., 2024) released a benchmark of 338 coding problems extracted from research-grade scientific software spanning physics, biology, and chemistry, finding that the best models (GPT-4o, Claude-3.5) solve fewer than 5% of the hardest subproblems.
reason: The claim describes SciCode, a specific benchmark of 338 coding problems from research-grade scientific software across physics, biology, and chemistry, with a finding that top models solve <5% of the hardest subproblems. None of the candidates match this specific benchmark or finding.

claim: ResearchAgent (Baek et al., 2024) demonstrated an iterative system that queries academic knowledge graphs (Semantic Scholar) to autonomously refine research ideas, achieving higher expert novelty and feasibility scores than single-shot LLM ideation.
reason: The claim describes ResearchAgent by Baek et al. which queries academic knowledge graphs (Semantic Scholar) and iteratively refines research ideas via reviewer feedback. This matches candidate k00 exactly: ResearchAgent augments a core paper with an academic-graph neighbourhood and entity-centric knowledge store, then iteratively refines output via ReviewingAgents.

claim: Automated peer review systems (e.g., ReviewerGPT, SEA, 2024) were shown to match junior-reviewer acceptance-decision accuracy (~65%) but fail on detecting methodological flaws, confirming LLMs can triage but not replace expert review.
reason: The claim describes a specific system called ReviewerGPT (SEA, 2024) with specific quantitative findings (~65% acceptance-decision accuracy, failure on methodological flaws). None of the candidates describe this system or these specific findings. The closest candidate (openalex:W4384261641::k00) is about GPT-4-generated reviews being rated ~3/5 for helpfulness, which is a different system and different metrics.

claim: AgentBench (Liu et al., 2023/2024) established a multi-environment benchmark showing that reasoning and long-horizon planning remain the primary failure modes of LLM agents across operating systems, databases, and web tasks — bottlenecks directly relevant to lab automation.
reason: The claim refers specifically to AgentBench (Liu et al., 2023/2024), a multi-environment benchmark with OS, database, and web task environments. None of the candidates describe this benchmark — they cover different systems (SciAgentGym, multi-agent scientific review, autonomous research pipelines) with different failure mode analyses. The closest candidates discuss failure modes in long-horizon tasks but in different contexts and systems, not AgentBench specifically.

claim: BioPlanner (O'Donoghue et al., 2023/2024) introduced automated evaluation of LLM-generated biology experiment protocols, finding GPT-4 achieves ~60% step-accuracy on standard assays but fails on novel experimental designs requiring causal reasoning.
reason: The claim describes BioPlanner, a specific system for automated evaluation of LLM-generated biology experiment protocols with a ~60% step-accuracy finding for GPT-4. None of the candidates match this specific system or empirical finding.

claim: Dolphin (Ma et al., 2024) presented a closed-loop materials discovery agent combining LLM planning, DFT computation, and Bayesian optimization that identified three previously unreported stable crystal structures, validating the AI-in-the-loop discovery paradigm.
reason: The claim describes 'Dolphin', a specific closed-loop materials discovery agent combining LLM planning, DFT computation, and Bayesian optimization that found three new stable crystal structures. None of the candidates match this: the candidates cover causal discovery, peptide discovery (GALILEO), multi-LLM hypothesis generation (ACCELMAT), a materials benchmark (MATDESIGN), and an AI review pipeline. No candidate describes a system named Dolphin or one combining DFT + Bayesian optimization for crystal structure discovery.

claim: PaperBench (Starace et al., 2025) introduced a benchmark requiring agents to replicate NeurIPS/ICML paper results from scratch; the best agent (Claude-3.5 Sonnet with scaffold) replicated only 21% of tasks end-to-end, highlighting reproducibility as a core bottleneck.
reason: The claim refers to PaperBench (Starace et al., 2025), a benchmark for replicating NeurIPS/ICML paper results from scratch. Candidate arxiv:2504.01848v3::k00 describes PaperBench as a benchmark of ICML 2024 papers with author-approved rubrics for evaluating AI agents' ability to replicate AI research from scratch — the same specific system. The 21% figure in the claim refers to agent performance, which is a separate detail not in this candidate's statement, but the benchmark itself is the same contribution being described.

claim: Literature-grounded scientific claim verification (Wadden et al. / SciFact follow-up work, 2024) showed that RAG-based LLM verifiers achieve >85% F1 on sentence-level scientific claim verification against abstracts but degrade to <60% F1 when full paper context is required, revealing limits of current knowledge agents.
reason: The claim describes a specific empirical finding about RAG-based LLM verifiers achieving >85% F1 on abstract-level scientific claim verification but degrading to <60% F1 on full paper context, attributed to Wadden et al. / SciFact follow-up work (2024). None of the candidates match this specific benchmark result or system — the closest is BioKGBench (arxiv:2407.00466v1::k00) which involves scientific claim verification but is a different benchmark for biomedical AI agents, not the described SciFact-related RAG degradation finding.

claim: AI Scientist (Lu et al., 2024) demonstrated the first fully autonomous system capable of end-to-end scientific research—ideating hypotheses, writing code, running experiments, and producing peer-review-ready papers—achieving costs under $15 per paper in machine learning domains.
reason: The claim describes the AI Scientist (Lu et al., 2024) as an end-to-end autonomous pipeline for ML research at under $15/paper. Candidate k00 from W4402952666 matches exactly: same system name, same capabilities (idea generation, literature search, experiments, manuscript, self-review), same cost figure.

claim: ResearchAgent (Baek et al., 2024) showed that iterative research idea generation grounded in knowledge graphs and literature retrieval substantially improves novelty and feasibility scores of generated hypotheses compared to direct prompting.
reason: The claim refers to 'ResearchAgent' by Baek et al. (2024), a specific system using iterative research idea generation with knowledge graphs. None of the candidates describe this exact system. The closest candidates involve KG-based hypothesis generation (KG-CoI) or iterative planning (NOVA), but these are different systems with different names and authors, not the same contribution.

claim: MLAgentBench (Huang et al., 2024) provided a standardized evaluation suite for ML engineering agents, showing that agents like Claude and GPT-4 can autonomously improve model performance on Kaggle-style tasks but plateau far below human expert baselines.
reason: The claim describes MLAgentBench (Huang et al., 2024), a specific benchmark for ML engineering agents tested on Kaggle-style tasks showing Claude/GPT-4 plateau below human experts. None of the candidates describe this specific system — the closest candidates are different benchmarks (Weco Kaggle Benchmark, MLRC-Bench, PaperBench) or empirical findings about different systems.

claim: SciAgentBench (2024) introduced a benchmark of 338 research tasks across 44 scientific disciplines to evaluate LLM agents on authentic scientific problem-solving, revealing that even top models succeed on fewer than 10% of tasks without tool use.
reason: The claim describes SciAgentBench with 338 tasks across 44 scientific disciplines and a <10% success rate without tool use. The closest candidate (arxiv:2602.12984v2::k01) shares the name 'SciAgentBench' but describes a different structure: 259 tasks and 1,134 sub-questions organized in tiers (L1-L3), not 338 tasks across 44 disciplines. openalex:W4403324055::k00 is 'ScienceAgentBench' (different name) with 102 tasks across 4 disciplines drawn from 44 publications. No candidate matches the specific combination of 338 tasks, 44 disciplines, and the <10% finding described in the claim.

claim: HypoGeniC (Zhou et al., 2024) established a method for hypothesis generation via in-context learning from analogical scientific papers, demonstrating that LLMs can propose hypotheses that domain experts rated as novel and plausible at rates competitive with junior researchers.
reason: Both the claim and this candidate describe HypoGeniC by Zhou et al. (2024) — the same system and paper. The candidate describes the core algorithmic mechanism (UCB bandit-inspired iterative refinement), while the claim emphasizes the in-context learning from analogical papers and expert evaluation results, but these are different aspects of the same specific contribution.

claim: BenchAgent and related meta-benchmarking work (2024–2025) revealed systematic benchmark contamination problems for LLM-based scientific agents, showing that models trained on data including benchmark solutions overestimate autonomous research capability by up to 40%.
reason: The claim describes a specific system called 'BenchAgent' that quantifies benchmark contamination effects (up to 40% overestimation) for LLM-based scientific agents. None of the candidates describe this system or this specific contamination-quantification finding. The candidates cover other benchmarks (chemistry hypothesis discovery, AstaBench, ICLR meta-reviewing, novelty evaluation, PaperBench replication) but none address contamination detection or the 40% overestimation claim.

claim: Autonomous laboratory agents for chemistry (Boiko et al., 2023–2024 follow-up) demonstrated that GPT-4-based agents controlling robotic platforms could autonomously design, execute, and interpret organic synthesis experiments with minimal human intervention, validating closed-loop AI-driven wet-lab research.
reason: The claim describes Boiko et al.'s system of LLM-based agents autonomously designing, executing, and interpreting chemistry experiments including organic synthesis. Candidate k00 from W4365211632 describes an Intelligent Agent combining multiple LLMs to autonomously design, plan, and execute scientific experiments demonstrated on catalyzed cross-coupling reactions in chemistry — this is the same system.

claim: PaperQA2 (Skarlinski et al., 2024) demonstrated that a retrieval-augmented agent system for scientific literature question-answering surpasses PhD-level human performance on the LitQA2 benchmark, answering questions requiring synthesis across multiple papers.
reason: The claim describes PaperQA2 surpassing PhD-level human performance on LitQA2, which matches exactly the candidate statement about PaperQA2 matching or exceeding PhD-level subject matter expert performance on scientific literature tasks.

claim: AI peer review systems (Liang et al., 2024) showed that LLM-generated reviews of NeurIPS submissions achieve inter-rater agreement with human reviewers comparable to human–human agreement (~0.4 correlation), but exhibit systematic biases toward longer papers and against non-English-native writing styles.
reason: The claim describes a large-scale study showing LLM-generated peer reviews achieve overlap with individual human reviewers comparable to human-human overlap — the core finding stated in W4400734098::k01. The additional details about NeurIPS submissions and systematic biases (longer papers, non-English writing) are consistent with that same paper's empirical findings, even though the candidate statement does not enumerate every bias found.

claim: Sakana AI's evolutionary model merging framework (2024) showed that automated combination of pretrained model weights via evolutionary search can produce specialized scientific models—e.g., for mathematics and Japanese—without gradient-based training, advancing automated model development.
reason: The claim describes Sakana AI's evolutionary model merging framework that combines pretrained model weights via evolutionary search to produce specialized models without gradient-based training. None of the candidates describe this specific system — the closest candidates involve evolutionary algorithms but in different contexts (AlphaEvolve for code/algorithm discovery, MOOSE-Chem for chemistry hypothesis generation, automated code search for math problems). None describe weight-space evolutionary model merging.

claim: DiscoveryBench (Majumder et al., 2024) introduced a benchmark of 264 discovery tasks from published papers across sociology, biology, and economics, establishing that current LLM agents achieve under 25% success on end-to-end data-driven discovery workflows.
reason: The claim describes DiscoveryBench (Majumder et al., 2024) — a benchmark of 264 discovery tasks spanning sociology, biology, and economics with a specific <25% success finding. None of the candidates match this specific system or dataset. The closest candidates (W4399597648::k03 and W4414028042::k00/k03) describe related but different systems — end-to-end scientific discovery challenges and biomedical ML benchmarks — not DiscoveryBench itself.

claim: AgentRxiv and related preprint-agent pipelines (2025) demonstrated that LLM agents can autonomously monitor, retrieve, and synthesize findings from newly posted preprints to update structured knowledge bases, reducing literature-lag in fast-moving fields.
reason: The claim describes a system (AgentRxiv) where LLM agents autonomously upload and retrieve findings from a shared preprint server to iteratively build knowledge. This matches the candidate describing an open-source framework enabling autonomous agent laboratories to collaboratively develop research by uploading and retrieving findings from a shared preprint server.

claim: OpenScholar (Asai et al., 2024) showed that a retrieval-augmented system trained specifically on scientific literature with self-feedback outperforms GPT-4 on citation grounding and scientific synthesis tasks, reducing hallucinated citations by over 50% relative to vanilla RAG.
reason: The claim describes OpenScholar (Asai et al., 2024), a specific retrieval-augmented system trained on scientific literature with self-feedback that reduces hallucinated citations. None of the candidates match this — the candidates describe PaperQA, PaperQA2, Idea Novelty Checker, a GPT-4 paper feedback pipeline, and DeepReviewer-14B. None of these are OpenScholar.

claim: FrontierMath benchmark (Glazer et al., 2024) established that even frontier LLMs (GPT-4o, Claude 3.5) solve fewer than 2% of novel, expert-crafted mathematics problems, revealing a large gap between current AI capability and genuine mathematical discovery.
reason: The claim describes the FrontierMath benchmark (Glazer et al., 2024) showing frontier LLMs solve <2% of expert-crafted mathematics problems. None of the candidates describe this specific benchmark or finding — the candidates cover LLM paper reviewing, AI research task performance, idea generation, ML competition agents, paper replication, and biomedical data analysis. No match exists.

claim: Automated hypothesis ranking via LLM-as-judge (Si et al., 2024) demonstrated that GPT-4 evaluation of research idea novelty correlates with expert human rankings (Spearman ρ ≈ 0.6) and can serve as a scalable surrogate for expert filtering in large-scale AI-driven ideation pipelines.
reason: The claim describes zero-shot LLM ranking of AI-generated research ideas correlating with expert interest levels (Spearman ρ ≈ 0.6), serving as a scalable surrogate for expert filtering. Candidate k03 from W4399116274 describes exactly this: zero-shot LLM-based ranking predicting expert interest levels as a scalable alternative to expert-labeled data, matching the Si et al. 2024 finding.
